meddic.metrics:
Revenue Growth: Achieve 30% year-over-year growth on their $28M ARR.
Operational Cost Savings: Eliminate the 2-3 full-time employees dedicated to manual order entry, representing a $100-150K+ annual hard-cost saving.
Dealer Adoption: Drive dealer portal adoption from a very low current rate to 80% or higher.
Order Volume: Successfully process the current ~1,566 orders/month with the capacity to scale by 30%+ without adding operational headcount.
Channel Revenue: The platform must support their current 85% B2B / 15% D2C revenue split.
meddic.economic_buyer:
Primary Budget Owner/Influencer: Yemilyn Mosley (CSO). She owns the revenue goals and the operational efficiency of the sales and digital teams. She is building the business case.
Ultimate Economic Buyer (Unconfirmed but Highly Likely): Hamza Deyaf (CEO). Yemilyn stated she needs to "present a unified picture—including cost and timeline—to internal stakeholders." Given the project's strategic importance and the CEO's noted frustration with brand presentation (pixelated images), he is the ultimate decision-maker with final sign-off authority.
Risk: We have no direct contact with the CEO. Our entire business case is being filtered through our Champion (Yemilyn).
meddic.decision_criteria:
UI/UX & Brand Presentation (Stated #1 Priority): The platform and partner must be able to deliver a world-class, modern look and feel that aligns with their brand leadership. The CEO's AI-generated website concept is a key influence here.
Acumatica Integration: The solution must provide a seamless, real-time, bidirectional integration with Acumatica to eliminate manual order entry. This is the core of the ROI and is non-negotiable.
B2B Functionality: Must natively handle their complex, revenue-based, multi-tiered dealer pricing structure without workarounds (i.e., no more Google Sheets).
Custom Tool Migration: The ability to strategically handle their 16 years of custom tools ("Rapid Ordering," "Build a Car") through embedding or rebuilding is a critical technical hurdle.
Partner Competence: The selected partner's ability to demonstrate deep backend expertise and a strong UI/UX portfolio is a primary decision factor.
Total Cost of Ownership & ROI: The business case must be clear. Yemilyn specifically requested an executive summary with ROI data points to present internally.
meddic.decision_process:
Partner Vetting (Current Stage): Yemilyn is leading the evaluation of 4 shortlisted VIP partners in parallel with platform discovery.
Partner Demos & Scoping: Each partner will conduct discovery and provide a demo, focusing first on backend capabilities and then on UI/UX.
Proposal & SOW Review: Yemilyn will receive formal proposals, timelines, and cost breakdowns from the selected partners.
Internal Business Case Presentation: Yemilyn will consolidate the findings, platform costs, and partner SOW into a "unified picture" to present to the CEO and other internal stakeholders.
Final Stakeholder Approval: The CEO will provide the final go/no-go decision.
Timeline: They want to move quickly to support growth, but no hard "sign-by" date has been established. A 4-6 month project timeline is the current expectation.
meddic.pain:
Financial Pain: The direct, quantifiable labor cost of $100-150K+ per year for 2-3 FTEs to perform manual data entry.
Operational Pain: The current manual process is a hard ceiling on their growth. They cannot achieve their 30% YOY growth target without hiring more data entry staff, making their operational costs scale directly with revenue.
Competitive/Brand Pain: The 16-year-old custom platform is slow, outdated, and does not reflect their market leadership position. This is a known frustration for the CEO.
Dealer Relationship Pain: The poor portal experience (no tracking, confusing pricing, errors) is causing friction with their 1,100 dealers, leading to high support volume and potentially impacting dealer loyalty and reorder frequency.
meddic.champion:
Primary Champion: Yemilyn Mosley (CSO). She is actively driving this entire evaluation. She understands the pain points deeply, has defined clear success metrics (80% adoption, 30% growth), and has explicitly asked for materials (ROI case studies, executive summary) to help her sell the project internally to the CEO.
Secondary Champion: Lorena Hernandez (Director of Digital Sales). She supports Yemilyn and is focused on the customer experience and conversion analytics. She will advocate for the solution that makes her team's life easier and improves the customer journey.
Potential Technical Champion: Charles Penn III (VP of AI). He will champion the solution if he is convinced that Shopify Plus provides a robust, API-first foundation for him to build his AI and automation initiatives on top of, rather than being a restrictive "black box.

feniex.strategy:
Core play is operational efficiency + dealer experience. Feniex is growing fast but operationally constrained by their current custom platform. The ROI story is: reduce manual order processing, give dealers self-serve tools, and integrate Acumatica so their ops team stops doing manual reconciliation.

Position Shopify Plus as the only platform that can do B2B natively at scale without a massive custom build. Competitors would require significant integration investment. We have the B2B feature set AND the partner ecosystem for Acumatica.

Champion Yemilyn has urgency — she's tied this to her revenue growth goal. Keep momentum, get SE demo scheduled, move to LOI before end of April.

risks.text: CEO rejects the business case — we never had access to him
risks.mit: Yemilyn filters everything to Hamza and we have zero direct relationship with him. If her internal pitch misses the mark on ROI or he has a pre-existing bias toward a competitor, the deal dies without us ever having a conversation with the EB. Priority: coach Yemilyn on the exec summary she needs and push to get a 30-minute CEO briefing before LOI.

risks.text: Competitor wins on Acumatica integration story
risks.mit: If a competing platform or partner positions a tighter, pre-built Acumatica connector than ours (e.g., native Acumatica commerce module or a certified BigCommerce + Acumatica partner), we lose on their #1 technical requirement. Jordan must own the integration demo — show the data flow end-to-end, not just talk about it.

risks.text: Partner SOW sticker shock kills momentum
risks.mit: 16 years of custom tools (Rapid Ordering, Build a Car, Fusion Line Configurator) will generate large SOW numbers from implementation partners. If Yemilyn sees $500K+ in partner estimates alongside our platform cost, the project gets shelved as 'too expensive right now.' Pre-frame the phased approach: Phase 1 = migrate + Acumatica, Phase 2 = rebuild custom tools over time.

risks.text: Partner vetting stalls — no one earns Yemilyn's confidence
risks.mit: With 5 partners in parallel, if none deliver a compelling demo or their proposals are unclear, Yemilyn has no horse to ride. The partner void becomes a project-stopper. Stay close to the partner process; if a frontrunner isn't emerging by mid-April, intervene to clarify the shortlist.

risks.text: We fail the brand and design check
risks.mit: The CEO's AI-generated website concept sets a high aesthetic bar. If none of our partner demos match that vision (too generic, too 'Shopify default'), we get disqualified on subjective UI/UX grounds before ever getting to LOI. Make sure at least one partner in the shortlist has a strong high-end UI/UX portfolio to show.

manager_q.compelling_event:
• Fy27 growth targets by end of year +30% in revenue
• 2–3 FTEs doing manual Acumatica order entry ($100–150K/yr hard cost)
• 1,100 dealers ordering by phone, email, and PDF — no self-serve portal
• Miss it: keep hiring to cover manual order volume; dealer friction grows; 30% YoY growth target at risk
manager_q.who_signs:
• Hamza Deyaf (CEO) — unconfirmed but highly likely the final sign-off authority
• Yemilyn Mosley (CSO) builds the business case and presents to Hamza internally
• We have NO direct access to Hamza — everything is filtered through Yemilyn
• Action needed: push for a 30-min CEO briefing before LOI
manager_q.urgency:
• Currently LOW — Yemilyn said Apr 16 she is tied up on other revenue projects
• Build it: every week of delay = continued manual order entry cost + declining dealer experience
• Frame: delay directly threatens their stated 30% YoY growth target
manager_q.risk_to_close:
• Yemilyn is distracted and deprioritizing — stall risk is real right now
• CEO (Hamza) not engaged — we are pitching blind through a filter with no fallback path
• Partner situation unresolved: originating partner vs. our recs (Conrad McDougall loop-in pending)
• Partner SOW sticker shock: 16 years of custom tools will generate large implementation estimates
• No LOI yet — deal is not formalized

meddic.metrics:
In-Store POS Revenue: $16.5M total annual revenue across 3 stores (Southern Concrete); ~$1.5M processed by credit card today.
Quarry/COD Credit Card Volume: $25–30M in COD concrete sales processed by credit card at quarry locations — potential full consolidation to Shopify Payments.
Store Count: 3 current locations, 4th standalone store planned.
Hard Implementation Deadline: Current ERP (readymix software) goes end-of-life December 2025 — POS must launch in conjunction with NetSuite go-live.
Decision Timeline: Logan committed to making a platform decision within 1–2 months of 3/20 call (~by mid-May).
meddic.economic_buyer:
Unconfirmed — Logan Hundley (Assistant Controller) is the primary contact but sits in finance ops and has stated he is not the final decision-maker for the store side. Kevin Fender (Store/Operations Manager) is the closest operational authority identified and was present on both the Technical Disco and Demo.
Amy Goudy (3rd-party NetSuite consultant) stated after the Demo that 'a decision is needed now to allow the parallel NetSuite project to move forward' — indicating there is an unnamed executive at Hedrick/Southern Concrete whose sign-off is required.
Risk: True Economic Buyer has not been identified or engaged directly. Logan and Kevin are both influencers, not confirmed budget owners.
meddic.decision_criteria:
NetSuite Integration (Non-Negotiable #1): All Shopify transactions must feed into NetSuite as cash sales with inventory sync. This is the primary evaluation filter — NetSuite's own team pointed Hedrick to Shopify as a preferred connector.
Modern POS to Replace ERP Checkout: Current process has cashiers opening/closing the ERP for every transaction — unacceptable. New system must be intuitive, tablet-based, and decoupled from the ERP.
Inventory Management: Real-time inventory tracking across 3+ locations, replacing fully manual processes.
Contractor/B2B Pricing Support: Must support varied contractor pricing lists (currently managed as coupons). Shopify B2B catalogues directly address this — key Plus differentiator.
Customer Data Capture: Must capture customer identity for COD/one-off credit card buyers to build historical purchase records (currently zero tracking for these buyers).
Payment Consolidation: Strong preference to consolidate all credit card processing (retail + quarry COD) under one provider — Shopify Payments is the path.
Competitive Evaluation: Hedrick is evaluating a short list of competitors alongside Shopify (specific names unknown).
meddic.decision_process:
Logan Hundley (assistant controller) is the project coordinator — he schedules calls, loops in stakeholders, and manages the evaluation.
Kevin Fender (operations/store manager) is the operational decision voice — most engaged in technical and demo calls.
Amy Goudy (3rd-party NetSuite consultant) acts as technical sounding board and integration validator.
Unnamed Executive at Hedrick/Southern Concrete — ultimate sign-off authority (not yet engaged directly).
Current stage: Demo completed 4/1. Next step is a proposal/commercial call (Chase + Logan to schedule, was due week of 4/7 — not yet confirmed).
Evaluation is competitive — Shopify is on a short list but competitor names are unknown.
No formal procurement or legal process has been mentioned yet.
meddic.pain:
Operational Pain: Cashiers use the ERP directly for checkout — a non-POS system being forced into a retail role. Entirely manual inventory, slow and error-prone.
Forced Urgency: Current readymix ERP hits end-of-life at end of 2025. They have a hard deadline to replace both the ERP (NetSuite) and POS simultaneously.
No Customer Intelligence: One-off COD buyers (credit card) are completely untracked. No purchase history, no customer records, no ability to identify repeat buyers.
Pricing Workaround: Contractor pricing is handled via coupons in the current ERP — informal, inconsistent, and unscalable.
Special Order Reporting Issues: Kevin Fender is reusing a single 'bulk sale' item code for all special orders, causing cost/reporting conflicts when costs change multiple times in a month.
Fragmented Payments: Credit card processing split across multiple providers (First Citizens reader at quarries, separate store setup) — operational complexity and no consolidated view.
meddic.champion:
Logan Hundley (Assistant Controller) — initiated the relationship, drives scheduling, and is coordinating the internal evaluation. Understands the NetSuite timeline pressure. However, limited operational depth — he defers store-specific questions to Kevin.
Kevin Fender (Store/Operations Manager) — the day-to-day pain owner. Most engaged on inventory, pricing, special orders, and fulfillment scenarios during the Technical Disco and Demo. Best positioned to advocate internally for Shopify solving real workflow problems.
Amy Goudy (NetSuite Consultant, 3rd party) — not a Shopify champion per se, but is a strong validator. She confirmed Shopify's transactions integrate cleanly into NetSuite as cash sales and is not blocking the deal. Her endorsement carries weight since she owns the NetSuite side.

hedrick.strategy:
Shopify came into this deal with a significant tailwind — NetSuite's own implementation team referred Hedrick to us as a preferred POS connector. That's the foundation: we're not selling against skepticism, we're confirming a recommendation.

The primary play is NetSuite integration + operational modernization. Their current process (cashiers opening an ERP at checkout, fully manual inventory, zero customer tracking) is visibly broken and has a hard deadline attached — ERP goes end-of-life at year-end. We don't need to create urgency; it already exists.

The Plus justification has to be built around B2B catalogues, not just credit card rates. Contractor pricing is currently handled via coupons — a clear gap that Shopify B2B catalogues solve natively. This is the angle Jordan Murphy flagged internally and the one Kevin Fender will feel most directly.

The quarry COD consolidation ($25–30M in credit card volume) is upside that surfaced organically. If Shopify Payments can own that processing too, the rate savings alone make a meaningful ROI case — and it simplifies their payment stack.

Immediate priority: get the commercial proposal in front of Logan and Kevin before momentum stalls. The Demo was 4/1 and Amy Goudy has signaled that a decision is a blocker for the NetSuite project. That pressure works in our favor — use it.

risks.text: Unknown EB kills deal on cost
risks.mit: Logan/Kevin are influencers — the true budget owner hasn't been engaged. If we land a proposal with them and the EB says no on price without us ever having a conversation with them, we lose with no recourse. Get EB identified and in the room before or during proposal.

risks.text: Competitor wins on NetSuite-native integration
risks.mit: If a competitor (e.g., a NetSuite SuiteCommerce partner or purpose-built construction POS) positions their NetSuite integration as tighter or simpler than ours, we lose on the #1 decision criterion. Need Jordan to clearly demonstrate our pre-built connector and own the integration narrative.

risks.text: Deal stalls into inaction
risks.mit: Proposal call is already overdue (was week of 4/7). If Chase doesn't get it scheduled immediately, NetSuite implementation momentum slows and Hedrick defaults to kicking the POS decision down the road — 'do nothing' wins by default.

risks.text: Lose on price — can't justify Plus
risks.mit: If the Plus pitch is just 'better credit card rates,' a savvy buyer downgrades to POS Pro or picks a cheaper competitor. Must anchor Plus value in B2B catalogues solving the contractor pricing coupon problem — that's the real differentiator Kevin will feel.

risks.text: Amy Goudy steers to a competitor
risks.mit: As 3rd-party NetSuite consultant, Amy is a key validator. If she has a prior relationship with a competing POS vendor or finds a cleaner integration story elsewhere, she could quietly de-position us with Logan/Kevin. Keep her warm and make her feel like the integration is built for her.

meddic.metrics: Wholesale floral distributor — 3 locations (Seattle area, Spokane, Boise). Majority B2B; warehouses operate like restaurant supply for the floral trade (customers push carts). Currently 10-15% of business done online across two channels: own in-stock portal (outdated) and a third-party floral marketplace. Majority of orders placed via phone/email/text with assigned sales reps. QuickBooks being stressed at current scale.
meddic.economic_buyer: Mark (last name not captured) — appears to be owner or senior decision-maker. He is personally evaluating platforms, has the authority to choose, and is the one with the backup plan. Grant Dugas (Shopify SDR) introduced us. No committee or other stakeholders mentioned yet.
meddic.decision_criteria: Unified system (everything in one place — prior pain from daily data sync failures). Online portal that is modern and usable (current one looks like 1998). Ability to handle advance orders — flowers sold before inventory and pricing are known. Support for both B2B wholesale customers (net 30/ACH) and retail (pay in advance). Sales rep order entry and tracking internally (not just customer-facing — this is a key short-term requirement Mark flagged as a common gap). Flexible payment processing (not locked to one payment provider). QuickBooks integration or path off QuickBooks long-term.
meddic.decision_process: Mark evaluating independently → SE technical call (30 min, late week of 4/7 or early week of 4/14) → Mark assessing platform fit from content care package + SE call → decision. No formal committee mentioned. Mark has a backup plan ready and is open-minded but skeptical — Shopify is one option he is exploring.
meddic.pain: Industry-specific platform being discontinued — drop dead date already passed (7 days before 4/8 call), timeline extended but urgent. Current online portal is 10+ years out of date — Mark calls it 1998-level, customers are patient but it is costing credibility. System cannot handle advance orders (flowers sold before inventory/pricing is known — this is a large portion of their business). QuickBooks is being stressed at current company size. Two siloed online channels (own portal + third-party marketplace) with no unified view.
meddic.champion: Mark is both the evaluator and the likely EB — no separation. Grant Dugas surfaced and introduced the opportunity. No internal champion advocating separately; Mark is driving the eval himself. This means there is no champion inside to coach — relationship with Mark is everything.

washington-floral.strategy: Platform discontinuation creates urgency but Mark has a backup plan and is not panicking. He sees Shopify as a long-term front-end play for online/customer-facing, not a full ERP. The SE call (next step) is critical — need to show that Shopify can handle: (1) internal sales rep order entry + tracking (his flagged gap), (2) advance orders with deferred pricing, (3) B2B + DTC + POS unified. If we solve the short-term sales rep order entry problem, Shopify becomes both the short and long-term answer. If we cannot, he uses a backup for short-term and potentially chooses whatever ERP integrates best for front-end long-term.

risks.text: Mark activates his backup plan before we get to SE call — urgency dissipates, deal stalls indefinitely because the platform discontinuation pressure is gone.
risks.mit: Move fast. Get the content care package out same day, get the SE call on the calendar within 48 hours. Create urgency around showing Shopify as the short-term solution too, not just long-term.

risks.text: Shopify cannot convincingly solve internal sales rep order entry and tracking — Mark told us this is the key short-term requirement that past platforms have failed on. If we fail this bar, deal is dead.
risks.mit: Prep Jordan (SE) specifically on this use case before the call. Research Shopify draft orders, B2B rep-linked ordering, and any app ecosystem solutions. Lead with a live demo of rep order entry in the SE session.

risks.text: Mark chooses a full ERP that bundles a front-end — if he goes with an industry-specific ERP that has a built-in online portal, Shopify loses both the short and long-term opportunity.
risks.mit: Position Shopify as the stronger front-end even when paired with an ERP. Offer to present a Shopify + QuickBooks or Shopify + ERP integration story. Do not concede the front-end decision by accident.

risks.text: Deal moves very slowly — Mark is thoughtful, has no hard deadline now, and is evaluating multiple options. Without momentum, this slips quarters.
risks.mit: Set a clear SE call date on the 4/8 intro call follow-up. After SE call, propose a specific next step with a date. Keep Mark engaged with relevant content between calls.

meddic.metrics:
Customer Base: Family distribution business serving local supermarkets, restaurants, hotels, and healthcare industry in Hawaii.
Current Order Volume: All manual (email, phone, Excel order guides, or written on napkins) — no e-commerce baseline.
Prospective E-Commerce Use Case: Independent businesses who need online self-service ordering with contracted pricing.
Implementation Target: 2027 — this is a planning-stage deal, not an imminent close.
meddic.economic_buyer:
Brendon Yamada — Executive VP, manages contracted corporate and group purchasing accounts. He initiated the inquiry, drove the intro call, and confirmed the company's direction. As EVP of a family-owned business, he likely has significant budget influence if not full authority.
Note: It's a family business — other family members or a parent company principal may also need to sign off. This has not been explored.
meddic.decision_criteria:
Online Self-Service Ordering: Independent businesses and smaller accounts need an e-commerce channel to place orders without calling a sales rep — the core use case.
Contracted Pricing Support: Customers have individually negotiated pricing by volume and type. The platform must manage multiple pricing tiers without exposing all customers to each other's rates.
Infor A+ ERP Integration: Their operations run on Infor A+, a distribution-focused ERP. Any Shopify implementation needs to sync orders and inventory into A+.
Hawaii Tax Compliance: Hawaii has unique wholesale vs. retail tax distinctions that complicate e-commerce setup — platform must handle this correctly.
Self-Service for Field Reps: Field reps should be able to place orders and view customer-specific pricing on behalf of accounts — a B2B rep ordering portal scenario.
meddic.decision_process:
Brendon Yamada is the primary contact and likely the internal driver.
Timeline: 2027 strategic implementation target — no urgency today. This is an awareness and planning stage deal.
Next steps from intro call: Chase to send content care package; schedule SE technical discovery call.
No competitive evaluation mentioned — Brendon responded to a Shopify marketing email and was engaged.
meddic.pain:
No Online Channel: Every order is manual — phone, email, Excel guides, or handwritten. As a distribution business, this is operationally inefficient and losing ground to competitors with e-commerce.
Field Order Complexity: Field reps don't have a clean tool to place orders with contracted pricing in the field. Orders end up on napkins or informal channels.
Missed Smaller Accounts: Independent businesses that want online self-service have no portal. Larger accounts (Hilton) use Punch Out / Bert Street — those aren't available to the long tail of independent customers.
Hawaii Market Complexity: The tax structure (wholesale vs. retail distinctions) has historically made building an e-commerce channel complicated — this is why they haven't done it yet.
meddic.champion:
Brendon Yamada (Executive VP) — reached out proactively, engaged constructively in the intro call, and confirmed a 2027 timeline. He is the internal driver.
Note: Grant Dugas initiated the relationship and is the primary Shopify AE. Chase is supporting.

kyd.strategy:
Long-runway deal. The 2027 implementation target is real — Brendon is not buying in 2026. The strategy is to stay top-of-mind and make Shopify the obvious, pre-evaluated choice when they're ready to move in late 2026 / early 2027.

Key things to establish now: (1) Confirm that Infor A+ integration is solvable — SE discovery call should identify whether a native connector exists or what custom build looks like. (2) Map the B2B pricing architecture to their contracted pricing model early, so there are no surprises at evaluation time. (3) Keep Brendon engaged with occasional outreach and relevant content, but don't burn relationship capital pushing for a faster close.

Grant Dugas is leading this account. Chase's role is supportive. Coordinate on outreach cadence to avoid over-contacting Brendon.

risks.text: Deal drifts through 2026 with no engagement and we lose to a competitor at evaluation time
risks.mit: A 2027 target means 12+ months of low urgency. If Chase and Grant don't maintain regular, value-add touchpoints, Brendon will evaluate whoever reaches out when the time comes. Set a light cadence (quarterly check-in + relevant content) so Shopify is the name in Brendon's mind when the planning window opens.

risks.text: Infor A+ has no clean connector — integration complexity kills cost/timeline expectations
risks.mit: Infor A+ is a distribution-focused ERP common in food service and janitorial supply. If SA discovery reveals that a Shopify–Infor A+ integration requires significant custom build (vs. a native app), Brendon may steer toward a platform more native to his distribution stack. Scope this early and get ahead of it.

risks.text: Large enterprise accounts (Hilton/Punch Out) need EDI/Punch Out support we can't cleanly provide
risks.mit: Brendon's major accounts order through Punch Out (Bert Street). If Shopify can't seamlessly handle those enterprise ordering flows natively, the platform solves only half the problem — which may not be enough to justify switching. Validate Punch Out compatibility in SE discovery.

risks.text: Hawaii tax complexity delays setup and launch
risks.mit: Hawaii's unique wholesale-vs-retail tax structure was cited as a past blocker. Shopify Tax needs to be confirmed as capable of handling Hawaii-specific tax rules. Chase or SA should pre-confirm this before Brendon starts expecting a smooth 2027 launch.

meddic.metrics:
Radio equipment manufacturer (products ~$1K+, often built-to-order with 6–7 configurable add-ons). D2C storefront (BigCommerce + WordPress) + B2G government business (quote-based only via gov.flexradio.com). On BigCommerce for ~15 years. ERP: SIM 7. Tech stack: QuickBooks, Klaviyo, Paymore, Hooks. Timeline: ~1 quarter out for implementation.

Key metrics to develop: cost of manual B2G quoting labor, revenue lost from no-backorder capability, dollar impact of 30+ day fulfillment cycles with auth expiry, cost of inventory errors and manual reporting.
meddic.economic_buyer:
Not yet confirmed. Contacts from Apr 15 intro call:
• Lori — Head of Sales & Marketing, Austin TX (HQ). Most senior title known; likely the EB path for a platform decision of this size.
• Michael — manages back-end/BigCommerce, Canada. Technical/ops contact.
• Andy — manages government/commercial side, Canada (~1hr north of Toronto). B2G operations manager.

Action needed: identify who owns the platform budget — Lori is the strongest EB candidate given her title, but this has not been confirmed.
meddic.decision_criteria: 1. Unified DTC + B2B + B2G visibility and reporting from a single platform. 2. Customer-specific portals — login experiences showing only relevant products and pricing per account. 3. Backorder handling — ability to place orders on out-of-stock items with expected dates (DX Engineering cited as the model). 4. Flexible, schedulable reporting with raw data access (backorders, cancellations, BTOC orders). 5. EDI support for B2G customers on SAP and other government procurement platforms. 6. Simplified tech stack — Andy's hard requirement: no app overload replacing BC's plugins. 7. Two-way SIM 7 ERP integration. 8. Serial number tracking and PO attachment for B2G quotes. 9. Functional customer-specific discount management (BC's is broken/abandoned).
meddic.decision_process: Day 1 intro call Apr 15 — on BC 15 years but actively evaluating (originally evaluated BC vs Shopify). Next: SE technical discovery call with Jordan Murphy week of Apr 20. After SE call → full demo. May loop in Paul/Frankie (Shopify) for SIM 7 systems integration. Andy will contribute 'outside-in, clean sheet' architecture input. EB not yet identified or engaged. Timeline: ~1 quarter out for implementation. No formal RFP or committee confirmed.
meddic.pain:
1. SIM 7 ERP one-way push to BC — no sync back; causes mysterious inventory adjustments.
2. No backorder capability — out-of-stock items cause customers to abandon or generate inbound calls.
3. 30+ day fulfillment cycles cause credit card auth to expire before capture; can't split payments on backorders.
4. Manual B2G quoting — Andy manages via BC backend; can't attach POs or track serial numbers.
5. No EDI support for B2G customers on SAP and other procurement platforms.
6. Customer-specific discount management in BC is broken — Andy gave up on it.
7. Reporting inadequate: Paymore lacks flexibility; manually pulling cancelled orders; Klaviyo/BC abandoned cart attribution confusion.
8. WordPress/BC password sync fails regularly.
9. QuickBooks address format misaligns with BC (company name split into first/last fields).
10. Heavy plugin dependency for basic B2B functionality.
meddic.champion:
Andy (andy@flex-radio.com) — manages government/commercial side; expressed strongest operational pain (broken B2G quoting, serial numbers, EDI, discount management). Proposed 'outside-in, clean sheet' architecture approach. Most technically engaged contact.

Lori (Head of Sales & Marketing, lori@flex-radio.com) — most senior title; the EB path. Needs to be cultivated as the executive sponsor.

Michael (mike@flex-radio.com, back-end/BC admin) — technical enabler, critical for implementation but not a champion.

No confirmed champion yet — Andy drives the evaluation, Lori is the path to budget.

flexradio.strategy:
15-year BigCommerce customer who is actively and openly evaluating. Day 1 intro surfaced extreme platform pain across 9 distinct areas — almost every key B2B requirement is broken or missing on their current stack. The play is to run a tight SE technical discovery call that maps SIM 7 ERP integration, backorder handling, and B2G quoting directly to Shopify capabilities — then land a full demo with the broader team.

Andy (gov/commercial mgr) is the strongest technical advocate; Lori (Head of Sales & Marketing) is the EB path. Cultivate both in parallel. Find the BC contract renewal date — that's the hard deadline that creates urgency.

anatomy.strategy:
CEO-level entry with Liz Huff as both the EB and the champion. She is actively engaged, asking the right questions, and has full authority to move forward. The play is to run a tight discovery call, map her pain to specific Shopify capabilities (site speed via Hydrogen/Headless, B2B catalogs, product management workflows), and build a business case she can act on quickly.

BrightPearl ERP evaluation is a parallel track — position Shopify as the commerce front-end that integrates cleanly with BrightPearl rather than competing with it. Mitchell Goff (Sage) made the intro and may be a useful partner-level advocate as the ecosystem picture becomes clearer.

meddic.metrics:
Website Revenue: $200K–$250K annually (current), targeting $400K — a 60–100% increase.
SKU Count: 28,000 SKUs to migrate, plus 4–5K product images.
Exploded View Diagrams: ~24 diagrams that are the core differentiating feature of the site.
Manufacturers: 20–40 manufacturer relationships with periodic price updates (some with 4,000+ SKUs each).
Budget Ceiling: ~$50K estimated (based on original BigCommerce build cost; Tim has no budget authority — boss decides).
meddic.economic_buyer:
Tim Rusniak's Boss (name unknown) — holds budget authority and makes the final vendor decision. Tim will narrow to 2 finalists and present to the boss with quotes.
Tim Rusniak (IT/Technical Person) — no budget authority. He is the day-to-day technical contact and vendor evaluator. His recommendation carries significant weight but he cannot approve spend.
Note: Tim is an influencer, not an EB. The actual EB has not been identified or engaged directly.
meddic.decision_criteria:
Exploded View Diagrams as a Theme-Independent App (Non-Negotiable #1): 24 hover-over diagrams where customers can identify part numbers, see part images, prices, required quantities, and add to cart — built as a standalone external app so it survives any theme change.
Smart Bulk Pricing Import Tool: Ability to upload manufacturer spreadsheets (~2,000 SKUs each), auto-match SKUs, update prices, and flag new/deleted items with a summary report. Manual 3-day price update process is the core operational pain.
Better Search Functionality: Current BigCommerce search returns dozens of results for specific queries — new platform must return precise, relevant results.
Theme Flexibility: The entire migration is motivated by being trapped in one BigCommerce theme. The new platform must decouple functionality from theme choice.
US-Based Agency (Non-Negotiable): Tim had a bad experience with an Egypt-based developer (American Eagle) — will not consider any agency that outsources offshore or operates outside US time zones.
Simple and Practical Approach: Tim explicitly does not want agencies that upsell accounting, inventory, or analytics systems. Straightforward migration + custom exploded view app.
Budget: Approximately $50K ceiling based on prior build cost (Tim's informal estimate — boss has final say).
Mobile-Friendly: Field operators use site on phones/tablets for in-field repairs — must work on mobile.
meddic.decision_process:
Tim Rusniak is evaluating 4 companies (Crimson Agility, ioVista, Netalico + 1 more), narrowing to 2 finalists based on BigCommerce backend review and quote.
Tim will provide BigCommerce admin access to finalists this week for accurate scoping.
Follow-up meetings scheduled week of 4/20–4/24 (Netalico confirmed; others pending).
Tim presents 2 finalists with quotes to boss — boss makes final decision.
No formal RFP or procurement process mentioned. No urgency or hard deadline.
meddic.pain:
Theme Lock-In (Primary Pain): Exploded view functionality built by American Eagle is tied to a specific BigCommerce theme. Changing themes deletes the feature entirely — the boss wants a new theme design, but every attempt loses core functionality.
Labor-Intensive Bulk Pricing: Price updates require 3+ days of manual preparation per manufacturer (matching 28K SKUs to manufacturer spreadsheets, reformatting for BigCommerce import). Any mistake creates upload errors. With 20-40 manufacturers sending periodic updates, this is a recurring operational burden.
Imprecise Search: Searching a specific SKU or term returns dozens of irrelevant results — field operators can't find what they need quickly.
Rising Worker Knowledge Gap: Older operators knew equipment from memory; newer operators need digital aids. The exploded view + parts recommendations are critical for field self-service repairs — a broken part with a 4-week lead time needs the full repair kit ordered on the first try.
meddic.champion:
Tim Rusniak — day-to-day contact, drives the evaluation, understands all the technical requirements deeply. He will make the recommendation to his boss. However, he has no budget authority and is not a traditional champion — he can advocate but cannot close.
Note: The actual economic buyer (Tim's boss) is unknown and has not been engaged. If Tim's recommendation doesn't land, there's no secondary path.

franklen.strategy:
This isn't a platform competition — it's a partner selection competition. Shopify is the only platform being evaluated. The deal is won or lost based on whether Chase brings the right agency partners who meet Tim's criteria: US-based, practical, no upsell, exploded view app capability, and a fair quote near $50K.

Three partners are already in motion: Crimson Agility (4/8), ioVista (4/10), Netalico (4/10). All are being evaluated in parallel. Chase's job now is to stay visible, facilitate the follow-up quotes (week of 4/20–24), and make sure at least one partner delivers a quote that Tim feels confident bringing to his boss.

Key risk: Tim is pragmatic and skeptical. He's been burned by outsourced developers before and has no patience for feature creep. Any partner that comes in over $70K or tries to sell ERP/accounting integration will be disqualified. The winning formula is: custom exploded view app (theme-independent), smart import tool, clean migration of 28K SKUs and images, stay at or below $50K.

The unknown EB (Tim's boss) is a wildcard. Tim will present the top 2 options, but Chase has zero visibility into what the boss cares about or whether $50K is actually the ceiling. Consider asking Tim to arrange a brief intro call with the boss before the final presentation.

risks.text: Boss's true budget is below partner quotes — all options get rejected
risks.mit: Tim guessed $50K based on a 5-year-old project. Custom exploded view as an external app plus 28K SKU migration will likely push quotes to $50–70K+. If the boss's real number is $30K, every partner gets rejected and the deal dies. Chase should ask Tim directly to gut-check the budget with his boss before the quote meeting so there are no surprises.

risks.text: We lose Tim's trust with a bad partner recommendation
risks.mit: Tim is direct and unforgiving. If any of our 3 partners come in with a high quote, an offshore team, or try to upsell an ERP integration, Tim will mentally disqualify Chase's judgment. One bad intro out of three poisons the well. Monitor the partner calls and give each partner a pre-brief on Tim's criteria: US-only, simple/practical, no upsell, ~$50K range.

risks.text: Boss doesn't engage and decision gets indefinitely deferred
risks.mit: Tim has no urgency (discovered the theme-lock in August 2025, waited 8 months already). If his boss has other priorities, the $50K decision keeps getting pushed. Tim's framing — 'the boss is getting frustrated with the dated look' — is the urgency lever. Coach Tim to use that frustration as the close signal when presenting options.

risks.text: A BigCommerce-native agency convinces the boss to stay on BC
risks.mit: BigCommerce is not completely out of consideration — BC did tell Tim they 'couldn't help anymore,' but a motivated BC partner could counter with a custom exploded view rebuild on BC. If that quote comes in cheaper and the boss doesn't understand platform-level differences, we could lose to inertia. Chase should ensure Tim understands the theme-lock is a BC architectural problem, not just an app problem.

risks.text: Deal drifts with no urgency into Q3/Q4 with no close
risks.mit: No hard deadline exists. The boss's frustration is the only urgency signal. Without a close date in Tim's mind, every follow-up can be pushed. Ask Tim to get a rough 'by when' from the boss so there's something to hold both parties accountable to.

bigrock.strategy:
Clean displacement of Cart.com with a full Shopify Plus + B2B + POS stack. Junior Campos is an engaged champion who has moved fast: three touchpoints in four weeks and a demo on Apr 23. The next critical step is partner selection (Apr 24–25) — Chase needs at least one strong agency finalist in the mix.

The Business Central integration is the technical anchor. Position ShipperHQ + Authorize.net as native ecosystem strengths rather than workarounds. Tom Arehart (Agency Partner Manager) is engaged — leverage him to ensure the right partner gets selected.

Economic Buyer engagement is a planned but incomplete milestone. Before LOI in late May, Chase must identify who signs and get them in the room.

risks.text: Economic Buyer not yet identified or engaged
risks.mit: Junior is Champion and Decision Lead but has not confirmed he holds budget authority. If a CFO, owner, or senior ops leader reviews and rejects on price without us in the room, deal dies late. Get EB identified and engaged before or during the Technical Workshop week.

risks.text: Partner selection goes wrong — wrong agency undermines confidence
risks.mit: Apr 24–25 is the agency shortlist decision. If the selected partner has no BC integration experience, poor B2B track record, or comes in with a sticker-shock SOW, momentum collapses. Tom Arehart must ensure a strong, qualified finalist is in the pool.

risks.text: Business Central integration complexity delays or kills SOW
risks.mit: BC ERP connector scope is not yet fully defined. If the Technical Workshop reveals the BC integration requires significant custom build, the SOW timeline and cost could push the go-live well past Q4 or exceed budget. Pre-scope BC requirements in the workshop and get a connector vendor engaged early.

risks.text: Cart.com retention play — platform offers concessions to keep Big Rock
risks.mit: Cart.com may get wind of the evaluation and come in with pricing concessions or promises of new features. Junior is a motivated champion, but if Cart.com offers a meaningful price cut or roadmap commitment, it could stall the decision. Quantify the platform limitations (not just cost) to make a stay-or-go comparison unfavorable to Cart.com.

risks.text: Timeline slips past late-May decision — Q4 go-live at risk
risks.mit: Every week of delay in signing pushes the build timeline and risks a Q4 launch window. Use the go-live date as a reverse-engineered close milestone and pressure-test it with Junior and the chosen agency.

bigrock.notes: Replacing Cart.com. Implementation team: Junior Campos (Champion), Sonny Rodriguez (Ops/Secondary DM), Evan + Conner + Nick (team). Shopify team: Chase McCane (AE), Jordan Murphy (SE), Tom Arehart (Agency Partner Manager). Quote expires June 6, 2026. Partner selection happening Apr 24–25. BC integration + ShipperHQ + Authorize.net in scope.

• Outgrowing Cart.com — B2B portal, pricing tiers, and ERP integration all hitting platform limits
• Business Central integration is manual and error-prone today — ops team wants clean order sync
• POS gap for Chicago location — needs unified stack
• Go-Live target Q4 2026 — reverse-engineered close timeline is late May
